content.text: What it is

Roadmap dependency sequencing identifies the prerequisite relationships between AI initiatives and uses those relationships to determine a build order that avoids blocking conditions. Horizon planning groups the resulting sequence into three time bands — Horizon 1 (capabilities that must exist before anything else, typically months 0-6), Horizon 2 (capabilities that require Horizon 1 foundations, typically months 6-18), and Horizon 3 (capabilities that require Horizon 2 maturity, typically months 18+) — providing a durable planning framework that is robust to delivery variance within each horizon.
Why it matters

AI roadmaps fail most commonly not because individual use cases are infeasible but because they are sequenced in an order that creates blocking conditions: a use case is approved and staffed before the infrastructure it depends on exists, requiring expensive rework, parallel rebuilds, or indefinite delays. Dependency sequencing makes these prerequisites explicit before the roadmap is committed, allowing the program to front-load capability-building investments (platform, governance, data quality) and sequence use case delivery in an order where each use case lands on a foundation that genuinely supports it. Horizon planning provides a communication layer that stakeholders can reason about — 'we are delivering X in Horizon 2 because Horizon 1 platform work is the prerequisite' — that is more durable than specific date commitments.
Benefits
Prevents blocking conditions: explicitly mapping prerequisites catches sequencing errors before resources are committed rather than after.
Enables parallel workstreams: dependencies that are independent can be identified and run in parallel, compressing the critical path without creating blocking risk.
Creates stakeholder communication framework: horizon labels ('Horizon 2') are more robust than dates for communicating to executives, since they survive delivery variance within a horizon.

Surfaces infrastructure investment timing: dependency analysis reveals exactly when platform capabilities (LLM gateway, MLOps, observability) must be available for downstream use cases to start, allowing precise infrastructure budget timing.

Enables portfolio prioritization within horizons: once use cases are assigned to horizons, the team can prioritize within each horizon by value, risk, or learning potential without violating sequencing constraints.
Trade-offs and limitations

Over-specification creates false precision: modeling every possible dependency produces a graph so complex it cannot be maintained; practical dependency graphs should model only hard blockers, not soft preferences.

Horizon boundaries are not walls: a team in Horizon 2 delivery may need to revisit Horizon 1 infrastructure decisions as they encounter production realities; the roadmap must have a mechanism to surface and resolve these cases.
Dependency graphs become stale: as the portfolio evolves (new use cases added, old ones retired), the dependency graph must be actively maintained or it misleads rather than guides.

Some dependencies are organizational, not technical: a use case may technically be ready for Horizon 2 but organizationally blocked because the target business unit has not developed the AI literacy to adopt it effectively.
How it works

Dependency sequencing begins with a complete inventory of planned use cases and infrastructure capabilities, represented as nodes. For each node, the team identifies which other nodes must be complete before this one can start — these are hard dependencies. Soft dependencies (preferences, not blockers) are documented separately and do not affect sequencing. The resulting directed acyclic graph (DAG) is then topologically sorted to produce a valid build order. Topological sort fails if there are circular dependencies, which always indicates a planning error that must be resolved before the roadmap is committed.

Horizon assignment maps the topologically sorted order onto three bands. The first set of nodes (those with no dependencies) is Horizon 1. Nodes whose only dependencies are in Horizon 1 are Horizon 2. Nodes that require Horizon 2 nodes are Horizon 3. This assignment is automatic given the dependency structure and provides the planning basis for investment sequencing: Horizon 1 investments must be funded and completed before Horizon 2 work begins, and Horizon 2 must be mature before Horizon 3 is resourced.

A critical sequencing principle: infrastructure must lead use cases by one full horizon. The LLM gateway, model registry, and observability stack must be operational in Horizon 1 to support Horizon 2 use case delivery. Programs that attempt to build infrastructure and use cases simultaneously in the same horizon consistently encounter blocking conditions and schedule overruns because infrastructure hardening takes longer than estimated and delays every use case that depends on it.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Roadmap dependency graph across three horizons

content.text: Arrows represent hard dependencies: an arrow from A to B means B cannot start until A is complete. Horizon 1 nodes (blue) have no predecessors. Horizon 2 nodes (green) depend on Horizon 1 nodes. Horizon 3 nodes (yellow) depend on Horizon 2 nodes. The graph makes the required sequencing explicit — agentic workflows, for example, require both domain teams and observability to be in place.
When to use it

Dependency sequencing is required for any AI roadmap with more than five distinct initiatives, or any roadmap that spans more than 12 months. Below five initiatives, informal coordination is sufficient. Above five, the combination of infrastructure dependencies, talent dependencies, and data dependencies creates a complexity that informal planning misses. Horizon planning is most useful when the audience for the roadmap includes executive stakeholders who need a durable framing rather than specific dates.
How to implement

Enumerate all planned nodes: list every use case and infrastructure capability the program intends to deliver. Include platform capabilities (gateway, MLOps, observability), governance capabilities (risk framework, RACI), and use case deliverables separately.

Map hard dependencies: for each node, answer 'what must exist before this can start?' Include technical dependencies (data availability, API readiness), organizational dependencies (team trained, sponsor available), and governance dependencies (risk tier assessed, security review complete).

Build the DAG and run topological sort: verify there are no circular dependencies. If circular dependencies exist, they indicate either a sequencing error or an implicit assumption that must be made explicit.

Assign horizons: map the topological order to three time horizons. Apply the 'infrastructure leads by one horizon' rule — ensure all platform capabilities are assigned one horizon earlier than the use cases that depend on them.

Validate with delivery teams: share the dependency graph with the engineers who will build each node. They will identify dependencies the planning team missed — particularly integration dependencies and shared data dependencies.
Review and update quarterly: the dependency graph is a living document; new use cases added to the portfolio must be placed in the graph before they appear on the roadmap.
Examples

A dependency health check can be automated and run before each phase gate to surface sequencing risks before they become blockers. The following script compares planned start dates against dependency completion dates to identify risks proactively:
Real-world example

A healthcare payer planned an ambitious AI roadmap with 18 use cases to be delivered in 18 months. A dependency analysis workshop (conducted before the roadmap was committed) revealed three critical blocking conditions: the member data platform (prerequisite for eight use cases) was not scheduled for completion until month 9; the model risk framework (required for all clinical use cases) had not been resourced; and three 'quick win' use cases shared a dependency on a claims data integration that was under a vendor contract renegotiation with an uncertain timeline.

The dependency analysis allowed the program to restructure the roadmap: the member data platform was elevated to Horizon 1 with accelerated funding; the model risk framework was assigned a dedicated resource in month 1; and the three claims-dependent use cases were moved to Horizon 2 with a clear trigger condition (contract renegotiation complete). The revised roadmap had 12 use cases in 18 months instead of 18, but each was sequenced correctly and delivered on schedule. The original 18-use-case roadmap would have delivered 6 on schedule and created blocking conditions for the other 12.
Common mistakes

Treating all dependencies as hard blockers: organizational preferences (a team would prefer to wait for a cleaner data integration) are not the same as technical blockers; over-constraining the graph delays delivery unnecessarily.
Not including governance dependencies: risk assessments, legal reviews, and security sign-offs are blocking dependencies that program plans frequently omit until they become delivery blockers.

Assigning use cases to horizons by business priority, not by dependency structure: a high-priority use case assigned to Horizon 1 that actually depends on Horizon 1 infrastructure will block until that infrastructure exists regardless of its priority assignment.

Building infrastructure and use cases in the same horizon: infrastructure hardening consistently takes longer than estimated; use cases that start before their infrastructure dependency is production-hardened encounter blocking conditions.
Not validating the dependency graph with engineers: the planning team knows the business dependencies; the engineering team knows the technical dependencies. Both are required for a complete graph.
Best practices
Apply the one-horizon lead rule for infrastructure: all platform and governance capabilities must be in the horizon before the use cases that depend on them.
Maintain the dependency graph as a program artifact: update it at each phase gate review with the actual completion status of each node and any new dependencies discovered during delivery.
Separate technical from organizational dependencies: map them in different colors or layers of the graph; they require different remediation approaches when they appear as blocking conditions.

Define 'done' for each node precisely: a node is complete when its success criterion is met, not when its delivery team declares it finished. Ambiguous completion criteria cause downstream blocking when a dependency is declared complete but the dependent use case cannot start.

Publish the horizon assignment to all stakeholders: executive sponsors, budget holders, and domain teams should all work from the same horizon assignment document, updated after each major planning cycle.
Performance and scalability

Dependency graphs scale with portfolio size: a portfolio of 10 use cases typically has 15-25 dependencies; a portfolio of 50 use cases may have 80-120 dependencies. At this scale, manual maintenance of the dependency graph becomes error-prone, and the team should move to a tool that supports graph visualization and topological sorting (project management tools with dependency features, or a simple graph database). The horizon assignment should be reviewed and potentially restructured when a major new use case or infrastructure capability is added to the portfolio.

Security and reliability

Security and reliability dependencies must appear explicitly in the dependency graph. Examples of security dependencies: the secrets management infrastructure must be in place before any production model deployment; the access control framework must be ratified before domain teams receive production access to the LLM gateway; prompt injection testing must be completed before any customer-facing use case is deployed. These are not soft preferences — they are hard blockers that the CISO's office will enforce, and their absence from the dependency graph means they will surface as last-minute blocking conditions during deployment reviews.
Key takeaways
Model dependencies as a directed acyclic graph: if the graph has cycles, there is a planning error that must be resolved before the roadmap is committed.

Apply the one-horizon lead rule: infrastructure capabilities must be assigned one horizon before the use cases that depend on them — building use cases before infrastructure is production-hardened creates blocking conditions.
Include governance and security dependencies: risk assessments, legal reviews, and security sign-offs are blocking dependencies that program plans must treat identically to technical dependencies.
Validate the dependency graph with engineers: the planning team knows business dependencies; engineering teams know technical dependencies — both are needed for a complete and accurate graph.
The dependency graph is a living artifact: update it at each phase gate with completion status and new dependencies discovered during delivery.
Horizon labels are more communication-robust than specific dates: stakeholders can reason about whether a use case is Horizon 2 or Horizon 3 even when delivery variance shifts the exact dates.
